results.possibleIdentity: Licio Isolani (born 1931) is an Italian-American artist and educator whose work is closely associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement in the late 1960s and early 1970s. Born in Volterra, Italy, he studied at the Florence Art Institute before moving to New York in the early 1960s. By the late 1960s, he had joined E.A.T., the organization founded by Billy Klüver, Robert Rauschenberg, and John Cage. Isolani was a participant in the major E.A.T. exhibition titled 'Some More Beginnings' (1968) at the Brooklyn Museum. His work often focused on kinetic sculpture and the use of modern materials like plastic. He notably collaborated with scientists and engineers, including experiments at Columbia University where he explored the use of atomic energy to provide permanent illumination for his sculptural works. Isolani also served as a Senior Associate Professor at the Pratt Institute's School of Art and Design for many years.

results.possibleIdentity: Stephen Beck (also known as Steve Beck) is an American video artist, inventor, and engineer who was a pioneer in the field of video synthesis and electronic art during the late 1960s and early 1970s. Born in 1950, he studied electronic engineering at the University of California, Berkeley, and electronic music composition under John Cage at the University of Illinois, Urbana. In 1968, Beck became a charter and founding member of the Chicago chapter of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), an organization dedicated to fostering collaborations between the arts and sciences. He is best known for inventing the Beck Direct Video Synthesizer in 1969, a device that allowed for the real-time generation of electronic imagery without the use of a camera. Between 1970 and 1973, he served as an artist-in-residence at the National Center for Experiments in Television (NCET) in San Francisco. His notable works from this era include 'Our Darling' (1969), an electronic multimedia sculpture created in collaboration with Paul Mohr, and the abstract video compositions 'Union' (1972), 'Cycles' (1972, with Jordan Belson), and 'Illuminated Music' (1972–1973). His work often focused on the synthesis of light, color, and motion, drawing influences from Op art and kinetic art.

results.possibleIdentity: Carlos Sansegundo (1930–2010), also known as Carlos Sansegundo Castañeda or Juan Carlos Sansegundo, was a Spanish painter and sculptor active in the late 1960s and early 1970s. Born in Santander, he studied at the San Fernando Academy in Madrid before working in Paris and alongside sculptor Henry Moore in England. He was a founding member of the artist group Grupo Ibiza 59 and later the Hondo Group. Sansegundo moved to New York in 1964, where he lived until approximately 1973. During his time in New York, he was closely associated with the Pop Art movement and developed friendships with figures such as Robert Rauschenberg and Andy Warhol; the latter served as his godfather when he married abstract artist Ruth Kligman in the mid-1960s. He is noted for his participation in the 1964 New York World's Fair Spanish Pavilion and for his work with modern materials, such as plastic. Academic sources explicitly link him to the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement, identifying him as one of the primary Spanish artists involved with or influenced by the organization's mission to bridge art and technology during the late 1960s. His work from this period transitioned from traditional sculpture to paintings featuring Pop Art elements and 'Hard Edge' abstraction.

results.possibleIdentity: Lorenzo Eaton (1815–1904) was a 19th-century artist, teacher, and businessman based in Concord, Massachusetts. He was born to Silas and Nancy (Stone) Eaton and was a prominent member of the local community, serving in the Social Circle in Concord and as a member of the Middlesex Mutual Fire Insurance Company. While he is historically identified as an 'artist and teacher' well-known to art lovers in Boston, his active period predates the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) era by several decades. Records from the Concord Free Public Library and genealogical archives confirm his involvement in decorative arts and teaching during the mid-to-late 1800s. There is no widely documented record of a contemporary artist by the name 'Lorenzo Eaton' active in the late 1960s or early 1970s; however, the description of an 'Eaton' associated with E.A.T. and biofeedback during that timeframe frequently refers to Manford Eaton (active late 1960s–1970s), a pioneer of 'Bio-Music' who used biological signals as artistic control data.

results.possibleIdentity: Marti Cohen (also known as Marti Cohen-Wolf) is a New York-based administrator and coordinator who was a central figure in the organization Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) during the late 1960s and early 1970s. She served as the Director of the E.A.T. Office in New York City, working closely with the organization's founders, Billy Klüver and Robert Rauschenberg. In this role, she helped manage the daily operations and coordinated the matching of artists with engineers and scientists for collaborative projects. She was also a staff member for the organization's publications, such as 'E.A.T. News'. In her later career, she became a prominent community advocate in Lower Manhattan, serving as a public member of Community Board 1 (CB1) for many years and advocating for projects like the development of Teardrop Park in Battery Park City and the Performing Arts Center at the World Trade Center site. She is also associated with a business entity called OBJECT:ART, LLC.
